skill.name: git-advanced
skill.description: Advanced Git workflow guidance for rebasing, conflict resolution, bisecting, reflog recovery, branch cleanup, and team branching strategies.
skill.summary: Guide and helper script for advanced Git workflows and recovery tasks.
skill.author: AutumnsGrove

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and blocker patterns, but most are Markdown Git examples or documentation false positives. The confirmed risk is a local Bash helper that can change repository state, delete branches, run rebases, and clear stashes after prompts; no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Executable Git Helper Can Mutate Repository State
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for executable command risk. The Bash helper runs Git operations that can delete merged branches, start interactive rebases, pop or clear stashes, and rewrite local workflow state. The actions are domain-appropriate and usually prompted, but users should review before running it on important repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is an executable Bash script and the cited lines directly run state-changing Git commands. Risk is medium because commands are local Git operations with user prompts, not hidden execution or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Documentation Shows Direct SSH Private Key Use
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for sensitive file reference, but low severity. The troubleshooting guide tells users to add a private key path to ssh-agent. This is normal Git setup guidance, but marketplace users should avoid exposing key paths or copying private keys into prompts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited documentation directly references ~/.ssh/id_rsa. It does not read or transmit the key, so the concern is guidance risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Network And Environment Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious network or secret access. The flagged URL and environment-variable examples appear inside conflict-resolution and history-cleanup documentation, not executable code. No evidence found of outbound requests that transmit secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are fenced documentation snippets about app configuration and removing API keys from Git history. They do not execute network calls or access live environment secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Command-Execution Patterns Mostly Match Git Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for the bulk Ruby backtick, shell substitution, xargs, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts. The skill is a Git guide, so many Markdown examples contain shell commands, commit hashes, reflog recovery, branch cleanup, and history inspection. No prompt injection text or malicious intent was found during contextual review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Sampled flagged lines are ordinary Git workflow instructions and fenced shell snippets. The one executable script is separately captured as a medium finding.

content.value_statement: Complex Git history, conflicts, and recovery tasks can be risky without a clear process. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for safer advanced Git operations.
actual_capabilities: Explains interactive rebase workflows for squashing, reordering, editing, and dropping commits.
Guides conflict resolution with ours, theirs, merge tools, and three-way diff checks.
Documents Git bisect workflows for manual and automated regression hunting.
Shows reflog-based recovery steps for deleted branches, bad resets, and lost commits.
Provides branch strategy guidance for Git Flow, trunk-based development, releases, and hotfixes.
Includes a Bash helper script for branch cleanup, conflict assistance, history viewing, backups, and stash management.
limitations: The skill cannot guarantee that history rewriting is safe for shared branches.
The helper script changes the current repository and should be reviewed before use.
Examples assume Git command-line familiarity and may need adaptation for GUI tools.
It does not replace team policy for protected branches, reviews, or release controls.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Pull Request History
use_cases.description: Clean local commits before review by choosing a safe rebase, squash, or fixup sequence.

use_cases.title: Recover From Git Mistakes
use_cases.description: Use reflog and backup branches to recover deleted branches, bad resets, or missing commits.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Branching
use_cases.description: Compare Git Flow, trunk-based development, release branches, and hotfix practices for a team workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Git State
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner safety review before running commands.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current Git status and explain the safest next command. Ask before suggesting any destructive operation.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Up Local Commits
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an interactive rebase for my last commits. Identify which commits to pick, squash, reword, or drop.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve This Conflict
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced merge or rebase conflict handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these conflict markers and recommend a resolution strategy. Explain whether ours, theirs, or a combined result is safest.

prompt_templates.title: Recover Lost Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced reflog recovery after a bad reset or deletion.
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through recovering lost Git work using reflog. Prioritize backup branches and avoid force pushes unless justified.

output_examples.input: I have five messy commits before opening a pull request.
output_examples.output: A rebase plan that keeps the feature commit, squashes small fix commits, rewords unclear messages, and creates a backup branch first.

output_examples.input: A merge conflict changed the same function on two branches.
output_examples.output: A resolution checklist that compares both sides, preserves required behavior, stages only resolved files, and runs tests before continuing.

output_examples.input: I accidentally reset away local commits.
output_examples.output: A recovery path that checks reflog, creates a recovery branch at the last good commit, and avoids overwriting shared history.

best_practices: Create a backup branch before rebasing, resetting, or cleaning history.
Use force-with-lease only when rewriting a branch that others do not depend on.
Run tests after conflict resolution and before continuing a rebase or merge.
anti_patterns: Do not rebase public shared branches without team coordination.
Do not run helper scripts in important repositories without reviewing their commands.
Do not paste private keys, tokens, or secret files into AI prompts.

faq.question: Does this skill run Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: The documentation guides command selection. The included Bash helper can run Git commands only when a user executes it.

faq.question: Is it safe for shared repositories?
faq.answer: It can be safe when users follow backup and coordination guidance. History rewriting on shared branches remains risky.

faq.question: Can it help with merge conflicts?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains conflict markers, ours and theirs choices, merge tools, and testing after resolution.

faq.question: Can it recover deleted commits?
faq.answer: It provides reflog and lost-found workflows that can recover many local mistakes when objects still exist.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes an executable Git helper script with state-changing commands. No malicious behavior was confirmed.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
